# Task Instructions: Project Documentation, Reports, Talks, and Tutorials

Use this file when the user asks for README files, licensing text, contribution guides, final reports, back-cover descriptions, talk slides, tutorials, onboarding materials, or public-facing project summaries.

## Core goal

Create polished, professional documents that explain the project clearly to instructors, reviewers, contributors, students, or conference audiences.

## Project framing

The textbook is an open-access, student-centered differential equations textbook built in PreTeXt/Runestone. It emphasizes:

- approachable explanations;
- short narrative blocks;
- frequent low-stakes checkpoints;
- interactive visualizations;
- optional audio narration;
- no-cost access;
- open licensing;
- instructor adaptability;
- modern digital textbook design.

## README and repository docs

A strong README should include:

- project title;
- short description;
- key features;
- repository structure;
- build instructions;
- licensing information;
- contributing guidance;
- acknowledgments;
- contact placeholders or author contact information if provided.

Use clean Markdown. Avoid formatting that breaks after code fences.

For build instructions, prefer concise command blocks and explain assumptions.

## Licensing

The project uses open licensing, commonly CC BY-SA 4.0 for textbook content.

When drafting license boilerplate:

- include attribution placeholders when needed;
- point to the license text without overloading every file;
- distinguish between textbook content and software code if relevant;
- keep chapter-file docstrings concise enough to maintain.

## Reports

For grant or final reports, use a professional but readable tone.

Emphasize:

- project goals;
- completed deliverables;
- textbook accessibility;
- sustainability;
- student-centered design;
- open educational resource value;
- PreTeXt/Runestone infrastructure;
- ancillary materials;
- future maintenance.

Avoid overclaiming evidence if assessment data is limited.

## Talks and presentations

For talks about the textbook or PreTeXt, the preferred framing is personal, practical, and student-centered rather than overly formal.

Useful structures include:

1. why the traditional textbook experience needs rethinking;
2. how PreTeXt provides pedagogical infrastructure;
3. what the differential equations book does differently;
4. examples of student-facing design choices;
5. lessons learned and next steps.

For a computational seminar or beginner audience, keep the tooling introduction accessible. Treat GitHub and Codespaces as practical setup tools, not the main intellectual focus.

## PreTeXt tutorials

For beginner PreTeXt tutorials:

- assume no prior PreTeXt knowledge;
- assume some LaTeX familiarity if appropriate;
- use GitHub Codespaces as the fastest setup path;
- keep GitHub workflow minimal and black-boxed;
- guide users toward building and previewing a small lesson;
- include local-install guidance as a later path, not the initial barrier;
- account for flaky internet or workshop setup issues.

## Back-cover or promotional descriptions

Keep descriptions to one polished paragraph unless asked otherwise.

Mention:

- differential equations;
- conceptual understanding;
- interactive or student-centered features;
- accessibility/no-cost access if relevant;
- confidence-building.

Avoid marketing clichés.

## IRB and research-adjacent documents

When discussing student survey or focus group materials:

- be careful about consent;
- distinguish instructional activity from research use;
- avoid implying data can be used externally without appropriate consent;
- mention alternative extra-credit mechanisms when relevant;
- be precise about time estimates.

## Quality checklist

Before finalizing, check that the document:

- has the right audience;
- is concise but complete;
- avoids unsupported claims;
- uses clean Markdown or requested format;
- preserves project terminology;
- gives readers a clear next step.
